skill.name: azure-ai-contentunderstanding-py
skill.description: Guides use of the Azure AI Content Understanding Python SDK for extracting structured content from documents, images, audio, and video.
skill.summary: Use Azure AI Content Understanding Python SDK examples for multimodal extraction workflows.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer reported many severe patterns, but review found no malicious code, prompt injection, obfuscation, weak cryptography, or certificate material. Most command and script findings are markdown or Python documentation false positives. The remaining risk is legitimate but material: the skill teaches package installation, Azure credential use, environment variable access, and sending content URLs to Azure cloud services.

medium_findings.title: Cloud Service Processing Of User Content
medium_findings.description: The examples submit document, image, audio, video, and invoice URLs to Azure Content Understanding with begin_analyze. This is expected for the SDK, but users must confirm they have permission to process the referenced content in Azure.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network calls are explicit SDK examples using URL inputs. The behavior is legitimate for this skill, but it clearly transfers user-selected content references to a cloud service.

medium_findings.title: Azure Credential And Endpoint Environment Access
medium_findings.description: The examples read CONTENTUNDERSTANDING_ENDPOINT from the environment and use DefaultAzureCredential. This is normal Azure authentication, but it can use local Azure identity context and should be limited to intended subscriptions and tenants.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The credential and environment access are directly shown in the code examples. I did not find evidence of exfiltration, but credential scope matters for safe use.

medium_findings.title: External Package Installation Instruction
medium_findings.description: The installation section instructs users to install azure-ai-contentunderstanding with pip. This is a standard dependency step, but it introduces normal package supply-chain and environment change risk.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The shell command is visible and intentionally documented. It is not malicious by itself, but it runs an external package installation command.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backtick Command Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The numerous Ruby or shell backtick detections map to markdown code fences and inline API names. I did not find evidence that the skill executes shell commands beyond the documented pip installation example.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are prose, table entries, and markdown formatting. They do not define executable skill code or a command injection path.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto And Certificate Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography and certificate or key detections are caused by ordinary words in descriptions, table text, schema examples, and key frame terminology. I did not find certificate material or cryptographic operations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed context contains documentation terms and sample field names, not algorithms, key files, certificates, or secret values. The static labels do not match the semantic content.

low_findings.title: Dynamic Import Finding Is A False Positive
low_findings.description: The script finding at the model import section is a normal Python from-import statement spread across several lines. It is not dynamic import, code loading, or runtime execution from untrusted input.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The import list is static and names Azure SDK model classes. There is no import expression, variable module name, or user-controlled value.

content.value_statement: Teams need reliable examples for extracting structure from documents, images, audio, and video. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through Azure Content Understanding SDK workflows.
actual_capabilities: Shows how to install the Azure AI Content Understanding Python package.
Demonstrates authentication with DefaultAzureCredential and an endpoint environment variable.
Provides examples for prebuilt document, image, audio, video, and invoice analyzers.
Explains how to read markdown, transcript phrases, timing, frames, and extracted fields.
Includes guidance for custom analyzer creation, analyzer management, and async client use.
limitations: Requires an Azure Content Understanding resource and valid Azure credentials.
Uses URL-based inputs in examples rather than local upload workflows.
Does not provide production error handling, retry policy, or cost controls.
Does not validate data residency, privacy, or compliance requirements for user content.

use_cases.title: Prepare Documents For RAG
use_cases.description: Convert PDF or Office document content into markdown that can feed retrieval pipelines.

use_cases.title: Extract Invoice Fields
use_cases.description: Create a custom analyzer that returns vendor names, totals, and line item details.

use_cases.title: Analyze Media Archives
use_cases.description: Transcribe audio and video, then inspect timing, summaries, and key frame descriptions.

prompt_templates.title: Start A Document Analysis
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for one document URL.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to show a minimal Python example that analyzes one document URL with prebuilt-documentSearch and prints markdown.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Prebuilt Analyzers
prompt_templates.scenario: Choose the correct analyzer for input media.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to recommend the right prebuilt Azure Content Understanding analyzer for documents, images, audio, and video files.

prompt_templates.title: Design A Custom Invoice Analyzer
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to design a custom invoice analyzer schema for vendor name, invoice total, and line items.

prompt_templates.title: Plan Async Media Processing
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to outline an async Python workflow for processing many media URLs with Azure Content Understanding.

output_examples.input: A request to analyze a PDF invoice URL.
output_examples.output: A concise workflow that selects the invoice analyzer, starts analysis, waits for completion, and reviews vendor and total fields.

output_examples.input: A request to process a video recording.
output_examples.output: A media analysis plan that uses the video analyzer and explains transcript phrases, timing, and key frame descriptions.

output_examples.input: A request to build a RAG ingestion step.
output_examples.output: A document extraction outline that turns file content into markdown and passes it to a retrieval pipeline.

best_practices: Use least-privilege Azure identities and confirm the endpoint targets the intended resource.
Verify content permissions before sending document or media URLs to Azure services.
Handle long-running operations, retries, failures, and cost controls in production code.
anti_patterns: Do not send sensitive content to Azure without privacy and compliance review.
Do not hardcode credentials, tenant details, or production endpoints in source files.
Do not assume sample code includes complete logging, validation, or exception handling.

faq.question: What does this skill help with?
faq.answer: It helps create Python examples for Azure AI Content Understanding analysis of documents, images, audio, and video.

faq.question: Does it require Azure credentials?
faq.answer: Yes. The examples use DefaultAzureCredential and an Azure Content Understanding endpoint.

faq.question: Can it process local files directly?
faq.answer: The examples focus on URL inputs. Local upload patterns would need separate implementation.

faq.question: Which prebuilt analyzers are covered?
faq.answer: It covers document search, image search, audio search, video search, and invoice extraction examples.

faq.question: Can it create custom analyzers?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes a custom analyzer example with structured fields for invoice extraction.

faq.question: Is it safe to publish?
faq.answer: Yes, with a medium risk warning for cloud processing, Azure credential use, and package installation.
